# Chapter 9 — Underlying Competencies (BABOK v3, selected)

> Source: **BABOK® Guide v3**, Chapter 9 *Underlying Competencies* (pp. 187–215).
> This file extracts the competencies that most directly affect the **quality of a requirements interview**: analytical thinking and problem solving, behavioural characteristics, communication skills, and interaction skills (esp. facilitation, listening, negotiation, teaching).

## 9.1 Analytical Thinking and Problem Solving (overview)

Core competencies: **Creative Thinking, Decision Making, Learning, Problem Solving, Systems Thinking, Conceptual Thinking, Visual Thinking**.

Key implications for the interview:

- **Creative Thinking** — generate and consider new ideas; explore changes to existing concepts; apply ideas to existing problems. The BA models this and asks questions that **challenge assumptions** to provoke creativity in stakeholders.
- **Decision Making** — gather relevant info, analyse, compare, identify the most desirable option. Effective decisions are made with the appropriate stakeholders, with pros & cons of all options clearly communicated, with rationale documented for future reference.
- **Learning** — quickly absorb new information; pass through acquisition → comprehension → application → analysis → synthesis → evaluation. Use multiple learning styles (visual, auditory, kinesthetic) for faster understanding and longer retention.
- **Problem Solving** — define the problem; surface stakeholder points of view and any conflicts between goals; identify and validate assumptions; specify objectives; develop alternatives; measure alternatives against objectives; identify trade-offs.
- **Systems Thinking** — a system as a whole has properties not predictable from its components alone; analyse the impact of any change on inventory, finance, training, etc.
- **Conceptual Thinking** — connect details to the larger context; link factors not easily defined to the underlying problem; generate alternatives.
- **Visual Thinking** — turn complex information into visual representations to engage stakeholders.

> The BA's job during the interview is to **think out loud across all seven dimensions while listening to one person at a time**.

## 9.2 Behavioural Characteristics

Core competencies: **Ethics, Personal Accountability, Trustworthiness, Organization and Time Management, Adaptability**.

Selected effectiveness markers:

- **Ethics** — prompt identification of ethical dilemmas; transparency in decisions; full disclosure of conflicts of interest; honesty about own abilities and errors.
- **Personal Accountability** — work is planned and articulated; status is known; risks and issues are identified and acted on; completely traceable requirements delivered on time.
- **Trustworthiness** — stakeholders involve the BA in discussions and decisions; bring issues to the BA; willing to discuss controversial topics; don't blame the BA when problems occur. Built by: consistently completing tasks on time, presenting confidence, acting honestly and addressing conflict immediately, maintaining a predictable schedule.
- **Organization and Time Management** — focus on the right tasks at the right time; schedule managed and communicated; meetings prepared for; up-to-date status on every work item.
- **Adaptability** — willing to change technique / style / method to fit stakeholder, organisation, situation; demonstrate positive attitude in face of ambiguity; alter interpersonal style across diverse individuals.

## 9.3 Business Knowledge

Core competencies: **Business Acumen, Industry Knowledge, Organization Knowledge, Solution Knowledge, Methodology Knowledge**.

Without business and industry knowledge the analyst cannot decide which questions matter, recognise when a stakeholder is wrong, or push back on infeasible asks.

## 9.4 Communication Skills

Core competencies: **Verbal Communication, Non-Verbal Communication, Written Communication, Listening**.

Planning communication: review what the receiver knows / does not know, structure the information logically, choose the format (visual aids, graphs, diagrams, bullet points), understand the recipient's expectations.

### 9.4.1 Verbal Communication

Verbal communication conveys information **and** emotional / non-verbal cues. The sender's choice of words and tone of voice carry meaning; when sender and receiver can see each other, non-verbal cues from both sides shape understanding. Pair verbal communication with **active listening** to confirm understanding.

Effectiveness markers:

- restate concepts to ensure all stakeholders share the same understanding;
- guide conversations to productive conclusions;
- deliver effective presentations;
- communicate an issue's important points **calmly and rationally**, presenting options.

### 9.4.2 Non-Verbal Communication

Non-verbal communication is believed to convey **much more meaning than words alone**. Moods, attitudes, and feelings impact body movement and facial expressions. Effective use of non-verbal communication presents a trustworthy, confident, capable demeanor.

When a stakeholder's non-verbal communication **does not agree with** their verbal message, explore further to surface the source of the disagreement — do not assume the words are the truth.

Effectiveness markers:

- being aware of body language in others — but not assuming complete understanding;
- intentional awareness of personal non-verbal communication;
- effectively addressing situations of verbal / non-verbal mismatch.

### 9.4.3 Written Communication

Effective written communication adjusts style for the audience, uses proper grammar and style, chooses words the audience will understand, and is structured so the reader can paraphrase and describe its content.

### 9.4.4 Listening

Listening is **not just hearing words** but understanding their meaning in context. Effective listening makes the BA accurate **and** shows the speaker that the BA thinks their input is important.

**Active listening** involves listening + interpreting what the other person is trying to communicate beyond the words, then summarising and repeating in different terms to ensure shared understanding.

Effectiveness markers:

- give the speaker undivided attention;
- acknowledge with verbal or non-verbal encouragement;
- provide feedback to confirm understanding;
- defer judgment; respond appropriately.

## 9.5 Interaction Skills

Core competencies: **Facilitation, Leadership and Influencing, Teamwork, Negotiation and Conflict Resolution, Teaching**.

### 9.5.1 Facilitation

Skill of moderating discussions within a group so all participants can articulate their views and recognise / appreciate differing points of view.

Effectiveness markers:

- make it clear the facilitator is a **third party, not a decision maker or owner of the topic**;
- encourage participation from all attendees;
- remain neutral and not take sides; intervene when needed to suggest or offer insights;
- establish ground rules (open to suggestions, build on what is there, don't dismiss ideas, allow others to speak);
- ensure participants correctly understand each other's positions;
- use meeting management skills to keep discussions focused and organised;
- prevent sidetracking onto irrelevant topics;
- understand all parties' interests, motivations, and objectives.

### 9.5.2 Leadership and Influencing

Motivate people to act in ways that achieve shared goals. Effectiveness markers include reduced resistance to change, articulating a clear and inspiring vision, inspiring others to turn vision into action, influencing stakeholders to understand mutual interests and consider broader objectives.

### 9.5.3 Teamwork

Recognise team dynamics; build and maintain trust; resolve conflict by examining positions, assumptions, observations, and expectations of all team members.

### 9.5.4 Negotiation and Conflict Resolution

Mediate discussions, help participants recognise differing views, resolve differences, reach conclusions that have everyone's agreement. Identify underlying interests of the parties, distinguish those interests from their **stated positions**, help parties identify solutions that satisfy underlying interests.

Effectiveness markers:

- planned approach considering tone of voice, conveyed attitude, methods used, concern for the other side's feelings and needs;
- recognise that the parties' needs are not always in opposition — both sides can often be satisfied without one losing;
- objective approach — separate problem from person; debate real issues without damaging working relationships;
- recognise that effective resolution often requires several meetings, not a single one.

### 9.5.5 Teaching

The BA confirms that communicated information has been understood by stakeholders. Lead stakeholders to discover clarity in ambiguity by helping them learn about the contexts and value of the needs being investigated. Select the appropriate visual / verbal / written / kinesthetic teaching approach for the information.

Effectiveness markers:

- use different methods to communicate information to be learned;
- discover new information through high levels of stakeholder engagement;
- validate that audiences have a clear understanding of the key messages;
- verify that stakeholders can demonstrate the new knowledge.

## 9.6 Tools and Technology (summary)

Core competencies: **Office Productivity Tools and Technology, Business Analysis Tools and Technology, Communication Tools and Technology**.

Relevant for interview support:

- **Office productivity** — word processors, presentations, spreadsheets, e-mail / messaging, collaboration / knowledge management tools, hardware (printers, projectors, scanners).
- **BA tools** — modelling, requirements management (workflow, baselining, approvals, change control, traceability incl. forwards & backwards, impact analysis, configuration management, quality checks), issue / item tracking, prototyping & simulation, CASE, survey engines.
- **Communication tools** — conferencing, shared whiteboards, remote collaboration platforms.
